analytic.title: Agentic Test-Time Scaling for WebAgents

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Test-time scaling has become a standard way to improve performance and boost reliability of neural network models. However, its behavior on agentic, multi-step tasks remains less wellunderstood: small per-step errors can compound over long horizons; and we find that naive policies that uniformly increase sampling show diminishing returns. In this work, we present CATTS, a simple technique for dynamically allocating compute for multi-step agents. We first conduct an empirical study of inference-time scaling for web agents. We find that uniformly increasing perstep compute quickly saturates in long-horizon environments. We then investigate stronger aggregation strategies, including an LLM-based Arbiter that can outperform naive voting, but that can overrule high-consensus decisions. We show that uncertainty statistics derived from the agent's own vote distribution (entropy and top-1/top-2 margin) correlate with downstream success and provide a practical signal for dynamic compute allocation. Based on these findings, we introduce Confidence-Aware Test-Time Scaling (CATTS), which uses vote-derived uncertainty to allocate compute only when decisions are genuinely contentious. CATTS improves performance on WebArena-Lite and GoBrowse by up to 9.1% over React while using up to 2.3× fewer tokens than uniform scaling, providing both efficiency gains and an interpretable decision rule.
* Equal contribution 1 UC Berkeley 2 ICSI 3 LBNL.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used not only to produce text, but to take actions: they can call tools, navigate websites, operate software, and execute multi-step procedures in interactive environments. In these settings, an agent must repeatedly choose a next action, e.g., clicking a Preprint. February 13, 2026. button, typing into a form, or issuing a search query, based on the current observation and the history of what it has already done (Patil et al., 2024;Schick et al., 2023;Erdogan et al., 2024). The sequential nature of these tasks makes reliability much more challenging than simpler single-shot question answering: a single poor decision can send the trajectory into an unrecoverable state, and small per-step error rates can compound over many steps (Erdogan et al., 2025).

A widely used strategy for improving LLM capabilities at test time is test-time scaling (Snell et al., 2024;Brown et al., 2024;Cobbe et al., 2021;Zelikman et al., 2022). The core idea is to spend compute at test-time generating more tokens, rather than spending this compute during pretraining. For single-shot reasoning tasks, this can yield large gains because different samples can explore different reasoning paths: even if the first attempt fails, additional attempts often contain a correct solution, and majority voting (Wang et al., 2023) or verification (Shinn et al., 2023)) can exploit this diversity.

An important question that remains to be answered is: What does inference-time scaling look like for multi-step, toolusing agents? A direct analogue of the single-shot recipe is to consider each step separately, treating each step the same way one would treat an ordinary reasoning task. At each step t, instead of generating one action, we can sample N candidate actions from the base model and then choose which action to execute. Repeating this procedure throughout a trajectory yields a simple and appealing "knob" for scaling up compute (Wang et al., 2023;Venkatraman et al., 2025).
However, a naive strategy, i.e., uniform scaling, where we always sample the same number of candidates and apply the same selection rule at every step, runs into two issues:

1. Wasted computation on easy steps. In many cases, a majority of steps are obvious based on the state and goal (e.g., continuing a form fill, clicking submit, etc.). Therefore, performing test time scaling for these easy steps results in wasted compute and higher inference cost.

2. High-Variance Decisions. Using majority voting when the votes exhibit very high variance and voting results are close is often not helpful. This particularly happens when the model has to solve a hard step, or when there are multiple plausible actions that can compete. In these cases, simply sampling more actions and taking a majority vote can be ineffective, especiallly when votes spread across many distinct options with no clear winner.

Recent work typically uses deeper rollouts to iteratively refine and improve solutions (Venkatraman et al., 2025;Zhang et al., 2024;Muennighoff et al., 2025). The most common pattern is to have an additional LLM that takes the current state and the list of candidate actions and then chooses the best one. We refer to this reranker-style mechanism as an arbiter.

While using additional LLMs can help with difficult decisions, they are also prone to overthinking (Cuadron et al., 2025), where even though the samples all agree on the best action, the additional selection step can override the consensus and choose a harmful action. In other words, extra compute is not automatically beneficial; it matters where and how we spend it inside the loop.

In this work, we study this design space systematically in order to understand where current inference-time scaling techniques are helpful and where they are harmful, in agentic settings. We begin by adapting several common inferencetime scaling techniques, such as best-of-N sampling and voting, reranking/aggregation via additional rollouts (Venkatraman et al., 2025), and confidence-aware filtering methods (Fu et al., 2025), to long-horizon web agents. We then analyze where these methods help, where they fail, and what signals predict those regimes. The outcome of this analysis is a simple principle: Inference-time compute should be allocated where it is likely to change the decision. Critically, we find that the distribution of answers generated at each step can be used as a measure for how likely the task will succeed (Section 4).

Using this insight, we present CATTS (CONFIDENCE-AWARE TEST-TIME SCALING), where we use the uncertainty at each step in order to dynamically allocate additional compute when necessary. We evaluate on WebArena-Lite and GoBrowse, and we find that CATTS achieves consistent improvements while using 2× fewer tokens than uniform scaling. This is accomplished by concentrating compute on uncertain and difficult steps, rather than spending it uniformly across all decisions.

div.head: Related Work
div.p: Inference-Time Scaling and Test-Time Compute. Vast amounts of recent work have explored how to improve the performance and reliability of LLMs by spending additional test-time compute. Self-consistency decoding (Wang et al., 2023) demonstrated substantial gains on reasoning tasks by sampling multiple chain-of-thought traces (Wei et al., 2022;Kojima et al., 2022) and taking a majority vote over final answers, treating it as an ensemble problem. Subsequent work has explored richer aggregation strategies including ranked voting and diversity-aware selection (Wang et al., 2025;Naik et al., 2023;Wan et al., 2024), although several studies highlight fundamental limits when sampled outputs share correlated errors (Byerly & Khashabi, 2024;Turpin et al., 2023).

Alternative approaches allocate compute via structured search over reasoning steps (Yao et al., 2023a;Besta et al., 2024;Zhou et al., 2024;Kim et al., 2024) or use confidenceaware filtering to gate computation (Fu et al., 2025;Kadavath et al., 2022). Recent work formalizes compute-optimal policies that trade off parallel sampling and sequential refinement (Snell et al., 2024), connecting to adaptive computation ideas in neural models (Graves, 2016). Another related line of work is DeepConf (Fu et al., 2025), which uses the model's own confidence scores to dynamically filter out uncertain and low-quality chain-of-thought. The tokenlevel probability distribution is used to compute confidence scores which can be used for confidence-weighted majority voting or filtering.

Our work applies these principles to sequential decisionmaking in long-horizon tool-use settings. We use votederived uncertainty to gate arbitration at each step, enabling dynamic allocation without requiring token-level confidence access. Unlike prior confidence-filtering approaches that operate on single-shot reasoning traces, we derive uncertainty from agreement among sampled candidates, and we operate online in environments where errors compound across steps.

Tool-Using Agents and Long-Horizon Tasks. Recent progress in building LLM-driven agents has been driven by frameworks that interleave reasoning and tool execution. ReAct (Yao et al., 2023b) demonstrated that intermixing reasoning with tool invocations improves performance, while subsequent work introduced structured decomposition such as decoupling planning from execution (Erdogan et al., 2025;Xu et al., 2023;Erdogan et al., 2024) or leveraging code for numerical reasoning (Gao et al., 2023b;Chen et al., 2023). To enable robust tool use, several frameworks teach models to invoke external APIs through self-supervised learning (Schick et al., 2023), retrieval-aware training (Patil et al., 2024;Qin et al., 2024), or structured integration layers (Karpas et al., 2022;Li et al., 2023). Complementary lines of work improve agent policies through reinforcement learning (Liu et al., 2018), iterative refinement via selfreflection (Shinn et al., 2023;Zelikman et al., 2022), or retrieval-augmented reasoning (Gao et al., 2023a). Evaluation of these methods relies on increasingly realistic benchmarks. For web navigation, environments range from early workflow-based tasks (Shi et al., 2017) to modern suites spanning e-commerce, forums, and content management (Zhou et al., 2023;Koh et al., 2024;Deng et al., 2023;Yao et al., 2022;Nakano et al., 2021;Drouin et al., 2024;Boisvert et al., 2024), while computer-use benchmarks extend to desktops and OS-level interaction (Xie et al., 2024;Rawles et al., 2023;2024;Zhang et al., 2025;Pawlowski et al., 2025). Multi-domain suites evaluate generalization across diverse tasks (Liu et al., 2024;Guo et al., 2024;Wang et al., 2024;Park et al., 2023;Murty et al., 2024).

Multi-agent debate and voting strategies (Du et al., 2024) and extreme decomposition via massive redundancy (Meyerson et al., 2025) offer alternative paths to reliability, often at prohibitive computational costs. Our work is orthogonal to these training-based and architectural improvements: we assume a fixed base agent, and we focus on how to allocate inference-time compute efficiently at test time. The central motivation is practical deployment, where unnecessary model calls translate directly to latency, cost, and energy use, making adaptive allocation essential for real-world viability.

div.head: From Static to Dynamic Inference-Time Scaling
div.p: This section presents the main study on inference-time scaling techniques for agents. We start from the most direct adaptation of inference-time scaling to web agents: sampling multiple candidate actions and aggregating them, progressively refining the selection mechanism based on empirical failure modes. We find that uniformly allocating inference-time compute inconsistently and inefficiently affects performance, and that the distribution of the actions themselves is an effective test-time signal for performance. This insight is used to propose a simple and interpretable inference-time scaling policy that dynamically allocates compute only when it is uncertain.

div.head: Experimental Setup
div.p: We study tool-using web agents that interact with a browser over multiple steps. The underlying agent is prompted with ReAct (Yao et al., 2023b) and uses gpt-oss-120b as the base model. At each step t, the agent observes a cleaned HTML representation of the current page state and outputs a structured action space of 8 tools including click, type text, scroll, etc. The full action space, agent prompts, conversation format, and error handling mechanisms are detailed in Appendices A and B.

We evaluate on WebArena-Lite (Koh et al., 2024), which has 165 tasks with programmatic success checks as well as 341 tasks sampled from GoBrowse (Gandhi & Neubig, 2025), where we use an LLM-as-a-judge (Zheng et al., 2023) evaluation using Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct (See Appendix C). We measure compute cost using the total token count per task, defined as the sum of prompt (input) and completion (output) tokens across all model calls, as a vast majority of the tokens consumed come from the input prompt. All results are averaged across three different seeds unless otherwise noted.
Action Clustering and Vote Distributions. At each time step t, the agent observes o t and samples N candidate actions from the base model:

We parse candidates into structured actions to obtain a set of action clusters A t . Let n t (a) denote the number of candidates assigned to cluster a ∈ A t . This induces a distribution over the sampled actions
which parameterizes how much the sampled candidates agree on each distinct action at step t.

One problem that arises when trying to do majority voting in this action space is that different outputs are semantically the same, but have different text representations (e.g., "N/A" vs. "Not found"). We therefore use a lightweight semantic deduplicator LLM to cluster candidate actions into semantically equivalent buckets before counting votes (See Appendix B for the prompt and Appendix D for additional analysis).

The central question of our study is this: Given the current observation o t and a multi-set of candidates { ã(i) t } N i=1 , how should we select the executed action a t to maximize task success under a token budget? We begin with static aggregation rules (majority voting and uniform arbitration) and then show how vote-derived uncertainty enables a dynamic allocation of computation. Figure 1 provides a visual overview of the selection strategies we evaluate.

div.head: Static Baseline: Majority Voting
div.p: The most direct adaptation of inference-time scaling to an agent loop is to sample multiple candidate next actions and execute the action with the most support. Concretely, after clustering candidates into A t , the executed action is
div.formula: a t = arg max a∈A t p t (a).
div.p: We refer to this as majority voting (or frequency voting). Most importantly, the compute allocation is uniform: the same number of candidates N is sampled at every step, and the same selection rule in Eq. 3 is applied throughout the trajectory.
Observation: Majority Vote Yields Diminishing Returns.

As we can see in Table 1, for both WebArena-Lite and GoBrowse, we find that simply increasing candidate-generation compute yields diminishing returns. Scaling from N=1 to N=10 improves success from 38.8% to 43.2%, but the doubling compute from N=10 to N=20 produces only 0.2% additional gain despite doubling tokens. We also evaluated Plan-and-Act style agents (Erdogan et al., 2025) with factorized candidate-generation budgets across plan and action sampling, and the results exhibits similar non-monotonic scaling behavior, establishing that the issue affects different agent architectures (see Appendix E for full results).

Takeaway. For long-horizon tool use, uniformly scaling up compute is often inefficient. Empirically, we observe that some steps produce highly diverse candidate sets, votes are spread across many distinct actions rather than converging on a clear winner. In these uncertain regimes, simple vote counting provides weak signal for selecting the correct action. This motivates selection mechanisms that can reason about ambiguous candidate sets, which we explore next.

div.head: From Voting to Arbitration
div.p: When majority vote produces diverse, uncertain candidate sets, a natural idea is to introduce an additional inferencetime mechanism that reasons over the candidate set. Concretely, after sampling N candidates and clustering into A t , we construct a short prompt containing the current observation o t and a list of representative actions (one per cluster).
An arbiter model then chooses the action to execute:
div.formula: a t = ARBITER(o t , A t , {n t (a)} a∈A t ).(4)
div.p: In all experiments, the arbiter uses the same model as the agent (gpt-oss-120b), and semantic deduplication is applied prior to selection. See Appendix B for implementation details.
Observation: Arbitration Improves Over Majority Vote.

Replacing frequency voting with an arbiter yields a consistent improvement over static majority voting across both benchmarks in our setting. Intuitively, the arbiter can use the Arbiter Scaling. Because the arbiter model itself is an LLM call, we can also apply inference-time scaling to the selection mechanism. With "Arbiter Scaling," we query K independent selectors and aggregate their chosen actions by majority vote:
div.formula: a t = arg max a∈A t 1 K K ∑ j=1 I SELECT j (•)=a .(5)
div.p: Empirically, increasing K yields further gains beyond a single selector call, and it consistently outperforms spending an equivalent token budget solely on increasing N under majority vote.
Observation: Arbitration is not Uniformly Beneficial.

Although arbitration improves average performance, we also observe regimes where an additional selector call reduces success, particularly when the candidate set already exhibits strong agreement. For example, in Table 2, increasing from K=10 to K=20 at N=5 causes performance to drop from 44.6% to 42.0%. Section 4 analyzes this failure mode in detail and shows it is predictable from vote-derived uncertainty statistics. See Appendix F for full results.

Deeper Aggregation Methods. We also evaluated Recursive Self-Aggregation (RSA) (Venkatraman et al., 2025), which iteratively refines candidates over multiple rounds. Despite substantially higher compute (up to 80 LLM calls per step vs. 10-11 for majority vote and single arbiter), RSA achieved comparable but not better performance than simpler methods. Thus, we focus on single-round arbitration for the remainder of our study; full RSA results are provided in Appendix G.

div.head: DeepConf-Style Confidence Filtering
div.p: We additionally evaluated DeepConf-style confidence-aware filtering (Fu et al., 2025), which leverages model-internal confidence signals to dynamically filter out low-quality reasoning traces during inference. DeepConf computes confidence scores from token-level probabilities and uses them to perform confidence filtering (retaining only top-η% traces) and confidence-weighted majority voting.

We experimented with three confidence variants adapted to our setting: (i) average trace confidence (mean across all tokens), (ii) tail confidence (final token segment), and (iii) bottom percent confidence (minimum confidence segment, focusing on the weakest part as a proxy for quality). Table 3 summarizes results across both benchmarks.

On WebArena-Lite, DeepConf variants achieve 38.8-43.8% success rates, with the best configuration (average trace at N=10) reaching 43.8%, improving over majority voting (43.2%). On GoBrowse, DeepConf achieves 86.6-90.3%, with average trace at N=20 reaching 90.3% compared to 88.0% for majority voting. These results confirm that confidence-based methods can benefit multi-step web agents. However, DeepConf requires access to token-level log probabilities, which limits applicability to API-only models where such signals are unavailable. In the next section, we explore an alternative uncertainty signal, derived purely from the vote distribution over candidates, that achieves comparable benefits while remaining applicable to any model that supports sampling.

div.head: Using Vote-Derived Uncertainty as a Test-Time Signal
div.p: We saw (in Section 3.3) that replacing frequency voting with an arbiter improves average performance, and that scaling the selectors can improve performance further (see Table 2). However, closer inspection of trajectories revealed a consistent pattern: the selector is most helpful on contentious steps, where multiple plausible actions compete, but it can be harmful on high-consensus steps where candidates already agree. On such steps, arbitration risks overriding a correct consensus action (see Figure 7). This motivates the main question of this subsection: Can we distinguish between regimes where arbitration improves decisions and where it introduces harmful overrides?

div.head: Analyzing Action Distributions
div.p: Given the vote distribution p t (•) over action clusters A t , we compute two uncertainty statistics:
div.formula: H t = − ∑ a∈A t p t (a) log p t (a),(6)
div.p: where a
(1) t and a

(2) t are the highest-and second-highestprobability clusters under p t (•). Intuitively, H t measures overall disagreement (higher entropy means votes are spread across multiple options), while ∆ t measures decisiveness (larger gaps indicate a clear winner). High-consensus regimes correspond to low H t and high ∆ t , contentious regimes correspond to high H t and low ∆ t .

Uncertainty profiles differ between successful and failed trajectories. We compare successful and failed tasks by their uncertainty statistics across steps. Let T be the number of steps in a trajectory, and define task-level averages

Empirically, successful trajectories tend to exhibit lower entropy and higher margins, whereas failed trajectories show the opposite trend. Figure 2 plots H t and ∆ t against the index of each step, separately for successful and failed runs, showing that disagreement typically spikes at pivotal decision points and that these spikes are more pronounced (or more frequent) in failed runs.

Uncertainty can predicts when arbitration helps versus hurts. The failure case in Figure 7 illustrates this distinction: at the pivotal step, the sampled candidates exhibit overwhelming agreement (vote split 9/10), corresponding to a large margin ∆ t and low entropy H t . In this highconsensus regime, the arbiter overrides the dominant cluster and chooses a minority alternative that derails the trajectory.
A frequency-based rule would instead execute the consensus action, which in our logs for this task, leads to success.

To quantify this pattern, we analyzed all 495 task-runs from our arbiter experiments, classifying each task by whether the arbiter overrode the majority vote on any high-consensus step (∆ t > 0.7). Figure 3 summarizes the results. Tasks without high-consensus overrides succeed at 46.9%, compared to 35.0% for tasks with at least one such override: a significant 11.9% difference (p = 0.026, Fisher's exact test). This effect exhibits a dose-response pattern: tasks with zero overrides succeed at 46.9%, those with exactly one at 36.6%, and those with two or more at 29.6%. The pattern is consistent across all five website categories.

Conversely, we find that arbitration provides benefit when uncertainty is high. Figure 4 groups tasks by average trajectory entropy and compares arbiter versus majority voting outcomes across all N configurations. At low entropy (0.0-0.3), the arbiter shows a net disadvantage of −4.4%, consistent with the override failures above. However, at higher entropy levels, the arbiter yields positive net advantages (+4-6%), demonstrating that arbitration adds value when votes are diverse and majority voting lacks a clear signal.
div.formula: U (ent) t = H t , U (mrg) t = 1 − ∆ t . (10
div.p: Both entropy-gated and margin-gated CATTS improved the accuracy-compute tradeoff in our experiments. For both settings, we tuned τ via running a simple grid over thresholds (e.g., τ ∈ {0.2, 0.3, . . . , 0.8}) and report results across this sweep (with the best-performing setting indicated where relevant).

div.head: Results
div.p: CATTS consistently improves over static baselines across both benchmarks. Figure 5 plots success rate versus total tokens per episode for majority voting, uniform arbitration, DeepConf, and CATTS. The key outcome is that CATTS achieves higher success than majority voting at comparable token budgets, while using fewer tokens than always-arbitrate and DeepConf for comparable success levels. At the best threshold (τ=0.2 for entropy-gated), CATTS achieves 47.9% on WebArena-Lite compared to 43.2% for majority voting, a 4.7% improvement. Averaging across all thresholds in our sweep, CATTS achieves 45.6%, which is still a consistent 2.4% gain that does not depend on threshold selection (See Appendix H for more analysis).

Because CATTS invokes the arbiter only on uncertain steps, it reduces the number of arbitration calls substantially. In our experiments, the selector is invoked on approximately 40-60% of steps on average (see Table 4), concentrating compute on the steps most likely to benefit. Notably, margingated CATTS achieves 47.9% success on WebArena-Lite using only 405K tokens while simultaneously reducing the number of tokens by 56% compared to majority voting (920K tokens). This efficiency advantage over DeepConf (which achieves 42.6% at similar token budgets) demonstrates that vote-derived uncertainty provides a practical and efficient signal for compute allocation.

div.head: Discussion
div.p: A key observation from our experiments is that inferencetime scaling behaves differently in long-horizon tool use than in single-shot reasoning. In our setting, per-step candidate sampling produces a distribution over actions, which falls into two different regimes.

Regime 1: Redundancy (high consensus). Many steps are routine, the next action is obvious and candidate generations concentrate on a single cluster (low entropy, high margin). In this regime, increasing the sampled candidates produces duplicates of the same decision. Figure 6 visualizes this redundancy by showing that a large fraction of steps have extremely high margins. Furthermore, additional arbitration introduces a distinct failure mode where it can override a correct consensus action (Figure 7). Together, these effects help explain why uniform compute allocation does not yield monotonic gains. See Appendices I and J for more analysis.

Regime 2: Contention (genuine uncertainty). On the other hand, a smaller number of steps act as trajectory pivots, where multiple plausible actions compete, and the vote distribution becomes diffuse (higher entropy, lower margin). These are precisely the steps where selection quality matters most, because a single wrong decision can send the agent into an unrecoverable part of the state space. In our logs, once an agent makes an incorrect pivot decision, returning to the correct path via backtracking or recovery is rare, as errors tend to compound rather than self-correct. In this contentious regime, arbitration can add value by using the observation to reason about plausible candidates.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: We present a systematic study of inference-time scaling for tool-using, long-horizon web agents. Unlike single-shot reasoning, naive per-step scaling does not reliably improve performance: many steps are redundant, while difficult pivot steps produce diverse, uncertain candidate sets where majority voting alone provides weak signal. We found that LLM-based arbitration improves decisions in contentious regimes, but it can override correct high-consensus decisions. To analyze these observations, we characterized perstep vote distributions using simple uncertainty statistics and showed that they correlate with downstream outcomes and identify when additional selection helps. We used this insight to propose CATTS, a dynamic inference-time policy that preserves majority voting when the model is confident and invokes deeper selection only when uncertainty is high. CATTS achieves consistent improvements across configurations and benchmarks while using fewer tokens, demonstrating that vote-derived uncertainty provides a practical signal for efficient compute allocation in agentic settings.
thermore, we appreciate the support from Google Cloud, the Google TRC team Prof. David Patterson, along with support from Google Gemini team, and Divy Thakkar.

Prof. Keutzer's lab is also sponsored by funding through BDD and BAIR. We also acknowledge support by the Director, Office of Science, Office of Advanced Scientific Computing Research, of the U.S. Department of Energy under Contract No. DE-AC02-05CH11231. MWM would also like to acknowledge DARPA, DOE, NSF, and ONR. DOE SciGPT grant. Our conclusions do not necessarily reflect the position or the policy of our sponsors, and no official endorsement should be inferred.

div.head: Impact Statement
div.p: This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field of Machine Learning. There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none which we feel must be specifically highlighted here.

div.head: B.4. External Planner (Plan-and-Act)
div.p: For the Plan-and-Act baseline, we use a two-agent system where an external planner generates high-level plans and a separate executor agent follows them. The planner's system prompt establishes its role:
You are a web-navigation agent that normally completes browser tasks by thinking in an analysis channel and then emitting exactly one function call (using the provided tools) per step.
In this role, you are acting as the Planner in a two-agent system:
-You see the overall User task, your earlier plans, the executed tool calls, and the latest HTML snapshot (inside the most recent tool observation).
-A separate Executor agent will later read your plan and actually perform the function calls in the browser.
For this planner turn:
-Use the analysis channel for deep, step-by-step reasoning and scratchpad.
-Use the final channel to output a clear, concrete plan for the next actions.
-Do not actually call tools in this turn; only describe how the tools should be used in the future steps.
The developer prompt provides additional guidance on plan quality:
Planner reasoning guidance:
• Think step by step and deeply in your own natural style.
• Focus on the next several actions and how to verify progress on the page.
• Ground your plan in: the user task, prior plans, executed tool calls and their results, the latest HTML. • Avoid unsupported assumptions about elements or states that you have not observed.
Recovery and loop-avoidance:
• Do not repeat plans or actions that failed or produced no state change.

• When something did not work, explicitly include recovery steps: different id, scroll, search, hover, go back, or another sensible control. • Clarify how the Executor should adapt if the page does not look as expected.
Referencing future actions:
• Refer to tools by their names (for example: click, type text, hover, scroll, select dropdown option, search, go back, exit), as they appear in the tool schemas.

• Describe how the Executor should pick the target element: use the integer id from the latest HTML when known, otherwise reference robust cues (labels, nearby text, attributes, structural context). • Predict how the DOM or URL should change after key actions.
• Specify what the Executor should look for to confirm success vs. failure (new headings, result lists, modals, disappearance of buttons, etc.).

div.head: C. Benchmark Characteristics and LLM-as-Judge Reliability
div.p: Benchmark difficulty comparison. WebArena-Lite and the GoBrowse-style benchmark differ substantially in task difficulty and evaluation methodology:

• WebArena-Lite (165 tasks): Uses programmatic success checks that verify specific page states or database entries. Tasks often require longer trajectories with precise multi-step reasoning. Average trajectory length is approximately 8-12 steps, and success rates are generally lower (40-47% in our experiments).

• GoBrowse-style (341 tasks): Uses an LLM-as-judge evaluation protocol. Tasks tend to be shorter and more straightforward, with average trajectory lengths of 4-6 steps. Success rates are substantially higher (86-90% in our experiments), reflecting easier task specifications.

The higher baseline performance on GoBrowse means there is less room for improvement from inference-time scaling methods. Nevertheless, CATTS achieves consistent gains on both benchmarks, suggesting the underlying principles generalize across difficulty levels.

LLM-as-judge reliability. For the GoBrowse-style benchmark, we employ the LLM-as-judge evaluation protocol introduced in the original GoBrowse work. Based on validation studies reported in that work, the judge achieves approximately 90% agreement with human evaluations on task success determination. This reliability level is comparable to inter-annotator agreement on similar web navigation benchmarks.

While LLM-as-judge evaluation introduces some noise compared to programmatic checks, the 90% agreement rate suggests that relative comparisons between methods (e.g., CATTS vs. majority voting) remain valid. The consistent improvement pattern observed on both benchmarks (WebArena-Lite with programmatic evaluation and GoBrowse with LLM-as-judge) provides additional confidence in our findings.

div.head: D. Semantic Deduplication Ablation
div.p: Table 5 demonstrates the importance of semantic deduplication for majority voting. Without deduplication ("Before fixes"), increasing the number of candidates N can hurt performance on GoBrowse: accuracy drops from 83.3% at N=1 to 80.1% at N=32. This counterintuitive result occurs because semantically equivalent actions (e.g., "N/A" vs. "Not found", or minor rephrasing of search queries) split votes, causing the majority vote to select incorrect minority actions.

After applying semantic deduplication ("After fixes"), scaling behavior normalizes and performance improves. For ReAct at N=8, accuracy increases from 83.3% to 84.5%. The per-website breakdown shows consistent improvements across domains, with particularly large gains on Reddit (84.8%→94.9%) where text input variations are common. This ablation addresses a potential concern that semantic deduplication artificially inflates results. On the contrary, the data shows that deduplication is required for majority voting to function as intended. Without it, the method exhibits behavior where more samples lead to worse outcomes. The improvement from deduplication (e.g., 80.1%→84.5% at N=8) reflects the restoration of correct voting semantics, and is not an artificial boost.

div.head: E. Plan-and-Act Scaling Results
div.p: We evaluated Plan-and-Act agents (Erdogan et al., 2025) with factorized candidate-generation budgets to determine whether non-monotonic scaling is specific to ReAct-style agents or a more general phenomenon. Let P denote the number of plan candidates sampled and A the number of action candidates per plan, yielding total budget C = P × A. Table 6 presents results on GoBrowse.
The results mirror what we observe with ReAct: scaling up compute via more samples does not reliably improve performance.

In fact, performance decreases from 83.3% to 80.6% when moving from budget C=1 to C=8. This establishes that the non-monotonic scaling phenomenon affects multiple agent architectures, providing additional motivation for the dynamic

div.head: F. Complete Arbiter Scaling Results
div.p: Table 7 presents the complete arbiter scaling results on WebArena-Lite across all combinations of candidate count N ∈ {3, 5, 10, 20} and arbiter scaling factor K ∈ {1, 5, 10, 20}. Success rates are averaged over 3 independent runs.

div.head: G. RSA and PlanRSA Full Results
div.p: We evaluated Recursive Self-Aggregation (RSA) (Venkatraman et al., 2025) as an alternative to single-round arbitration. RSA iteratively refines candidates over T rounds, with K aggregation calls per round. Building ontop of Plan-and-Act, we combine it with RSA to create PlanRSA, where we apply RSA at the plan level. Table 8 presents results across all configurations tested on WebArena-Lite. (2) Bimodal entropy: The normalized entropy distribution is bimodal (mean 0.474), with approximately 40% of steps at zero entropy (perfect consensus) and approximately 49% showing entropy above 0.6. (3) Two-regime interpretation: The distributions strongly support the two-regime interpretation from Section 4.4: a large fraction of steps are routine (high consensus) while a comparable fraction are contentious (genuine uncertainty), motivating dynamic compute allocation.

These empirical profiles directly motivate the dynamic gating approach in CATTS: by allocating extra arbitration compute to the high-entropy tail while preserving consensus on the dominant low-entropy steps, we can improve the accuracy-compute tradeoff without the overhead of uniform scaling.

div.head: J. Failure Node Examples
div.p: This section provides a concrete example from our experiments where the arbiter's override of high-consensus decisions led to task failure. Theis example illustrate the core failure mode discussed in Section 4: when the vote distribution already exhibits strong agreement (high margin ∆ t , low entropy H t ), arbitration introduces override risk rather than improving decision quality. Figure 7 presents an example from a WebArena-Lite task requiring the agent to find "Meat Substitutes" in an online grocery store. At a critical decision point, the sampled candidates show 90% consensus for "Scroll Down", the correct action, since the target category lies below the current viewport. However, the arbiter overrides this strong consensus and selects "Click Pantry Staples," a minority action that navigates to an incorrect category and derails the trajectory.

This failure pattern is particularly instructive because the vote distribution provides a clear signal: with 9 out of 10 candidates agreeing on "Scroll," the normalized entropy is low (H t ≈ 0.3) and the margin is high (∆ t = 0.6). Under CATTS, this step would be classified as high-confidence and the arbiter would be bypassed entirely, preserving the consensus action. The example thus demonstrates both (i) why uniform arbitration can be harmful, and (ii) how vote-derived uncertainty enables selective intervention.

figure.head: Figure 1 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 1. Comparing agentic inference-time scaling methods. Visual comparison of selection strategies at each agent step. Left: Majority Voting samples N candidates and selects the most frequent action via argmax over vote distribution p t (a). Center-Left: Arbiter samples N candidates and uses an additional LLM call to reason over candidates and select the best action. Center-Right: CATTS conditionally invokes the arbiter only when vote-derived uncertainty (entropy H t or margin ∆ t ) exceeds threshold τ, otherwise falls back to majority voting.

figure.head: Majority Vote Arbiter Confidence-Aware Test-Time Scaling (CATTS)
Table 1 .
figure.figDesc: Static inference-time scaling with majority vote. Success rates on WebArena-Lite (WA) and GoBrowse (GB) under varying candidate counts N. Scaling from N=1 to N=20 yields diminishing returns: on WebArena-Lite, the final doubling (N=10 → N=20) produces negligible gain despite 2× tokens; on GoBrowse, gains are smaller and non-monotonic. This motivates stronger selection mechanisms beyond simple majority voting.

row.cell: Comparison of majority voting, single arbiter call (K=1), and Arbiter Scaling (K>1) on both benchmarks. On WebArena-Lite, Arbiter Scaling improves from 42.8% (K=1) to 44.6% (K=10), a +1.8% gain, but K=20 degrades to 42.0%. On GoBrowse, scaling yields steady improvement from 88.6% (K=1) to 89.6% (K=20). Success rates are averaged over 3 runs.
Majority vote (N=5) Arbiter (K=1) Arbiter scaling (K=5) Arbiter scaling (K=10) 44.6% 42.4% 42.8% 44.2% Arbiter scaling (K=20) 42.0%

observation context to break ties among plausible actions and to discard candidates that look superficially popular but are contextually inappropriate. Table 2 summarizes the comparison between (i) majority vote, (ii) a single arbiter call per step, and (iii) scaled arbitration described below.

figure.head: Table 3 .
figure.figDesc: DeepConf variants on WebArena-Lite and GoBrowse. Success rates for three confidence estimation methods across varying candidate counts N. All variants can improve over majority voting baselines (WA: 43.2%, GB: 88.0% at N=10). Average trace achieves 43.8% on WA at N=10, while on GoBrowse, average trace at N=20 reaches 90.3%. Unlike CATTS, DeepConf requires access to token-level log probabilities, limiting applicability for API-only models.

High-consensus override analysis. Task success rate decreases as the number of high-consensus overrides (∆ t > 0.7) increases, showing a dose-response pattern. The red dashed line indicates the overall success rate (44.0%). The effect is significant (p = 0.026, Fisher's exact test) and consistent across all websites.

row.cell: Figure 4. Arbiter effectiveness varies with uncertainty. Tasks are grouped by average trajectory entropy and evaluated under both arbiter and majority voting, aggregated across all runs. Net advantage measures the difference in win rates: positive values indicate that arbitration succeeds on more tasks where majority voting fails than vice versa. At low entropy, arbitration provides no benefit and can hurt performance (−4.4%). At higher entropy lev-els, arbitration consistently outperforms majority voting (+4-6%), demonstrating that its reasoning capabilities are most valuable when the candidate distribution lacks a clear signal.

and H t is low, the model is already confident, and arbi-tration introduces override risk, rather than improving the decision; conversely, when entropy is high and votes are diverse, arbitration can reason over the candidate set and improve selection. This motivates a dynamic allocation rule: invoke arbitration primarily when vote-derived uncertainty indicates that the decision is genuinely contentious.
We use vote-derived uncertainty to decide whether to spend extra compute on arbitration at each step. Let U t be a scalar uncertainty score computed from the vote distribution p t (•).
These findings illustrate a core principle: when ∆ t is high

figure.note: CATTS applies a threshold τ to gate arbitration:a t = arg max a p t (a), U t ≤ τ ARBITER(o t , A t , {n t (a)} a∈A t ), U t > τ. (9)

figure.head: Table 4 .
figure.figDesc: CATTS results. We report success rates and token usage for majority vote, always-arbitrate, and CATTS at N=10.

row.cell: CATTS with entropy gating (H; τ=0.2 for WebArena-Lite, τ=0.5 for GoBrowse) achieves 47.9% and 90.2% respectively, resulting in a 4.7% and 2.2% gain over majority vote. Margin-gated CATTS (∆) achieves similar performance on GoBrowse (90.4%) while using only 405K tokens (56% reduction vs. major-ity vote). Both variants substantially outperform always-arbitrate baselines.
Majority vote Always-arbitrate CATTS (H, best τ) 47.9% 43.2% 44.0% CATTS (∆, best τ) 47.9%
We instantiate U t in two effective variants:

figure.figDesc: GoBrowseFigure5. Accuracy-compute frontier across all methods. Success rate versus total tokens per episode on WebArena-Lite (left) and GoBrowse (right). Each point represents a different configuration: Majority Vote varies N ∈ {1, 3, 5, 10, 20}; Arbiter shows K=1 (one Arbiter used) with varying N; Arbiter Scaling shows increasing K at fixed N=5; CATTS (Entropy/Margin) sweeps thresholds τ at N=10; DeepConf varies N ∈ {3, 5, 10, 20}. CATTS achieves Pareto improvements: on WebArena-Lite, it reaches 47.9% success at ∼750K tokens (vs. 43.2% for Majority Vote at 920K tokens). DeepConf also performs strongly, achieving competitive accuracy at lower token budgets than majority vote.

figure.head: Table 5 .
figure.figDesc: Impact of semantic deduplication on GoBrowse. Without deduplication, majority voting degrades with more samples due to vote splitting among semantically equivalent actions. With deduplication, expected scaling behavior is restored and performance improves. This ablation confirms that semantic deduplication is a necessary preprocessing step for meaningful vote aggregation, not a confounding factor.

figure.head: Table 6 .
figure.figDesc: Plan-and-Act scaling on WebArena-Lite and GoBrowse. Success rates under different budget allocations (P, A) with total compute C = P × A. Non-monotonic scaling is evident on both benchmarks: on WebArena-Lite, (2, 4) achieves the best result (43.2%) but (4, 4) drops to 43.0% despite 2× the budget; on GoBrowse, the baseline (1, 1) achieves 83.3%, while (2, 4) with 8× the budget drops to 80.6%. This confirms that the non-monotonic scaling phenomenon affects multiple agent architectures, not just ReAct, strengthening the motivation for dynamic compute allocation.

row.cell: compute allocation approach developed in Section 4.2.

figure.head: Table 7 .
figure.figDesc: Complete arbiter scaling on WebArena-Lite. Success rates for all (N, K) configurations, averaged over 3 runs. Best result for each N is bolded. The optimal arbiter scaling factor K varies by candidate count N: small N benefits from high K (N=3, K=20: 45.7%), while larger N prefers moderate K (N=10, K=5: 44.6%). No single (N, K) dominates, and gains from arbiter scaling plateau or decline beyond optimal K. This non-monotonic behavior motivates selective arbiter use via dynamic gating rather than uniform high-K allocation.

figure.head: Table 8 .
figure.figDesc: Full RSA and PlanRSA results on WebArena-Lite. RSA applies iterative refinement at the action level, while PlanRSA applies it at the plan level for Plan-and-Act agents. Despite substantially higher compute costs (up to 80 LLM calls per step), RSA achieves at best comparable performance to single-round arbitration. PlanRSA underperforms all baselines, suggesting that aggregation noise accumulates more severely at higher levels of abstraction. Figure6. Vote distribution profiles across all steps. Histograms aggregated over all decision steps from N=10 experiments on WebArena-Lite. (A) Top-1 probability distribution shows strong right skew: ∼42% of steps have near-deterministic consensus (top-1 probability >0.9), and mean top-1 probability is 0.762. This indicates many steps fall into a redundancy regime where additional candidates duplicate the dominant action. (B) Normalized entropy distribution is bimodal (mean 0.474): ∼40% of steps have zero entropy (perfect consensus), while ∼49% of steps have entropy >0.6, identifying contentious steps where arbitration is most valuable. These profiles motivate dynamic gating: allocate extra compute to high-entropy steps while preserving consensus elsewhere.

row.cell: Baselines Majority Vote Arbiter (K=1) CATTS (entropy, best) RSA (action-level aggregation) 10 10 10 RSA 8 RSA 8 RSA 16 RSA 16
PlanRSA (plan-level aggregation) PlanRSA (arbiter select) 8 PlanRSA (random select) 8

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: We acknowledge the gracious support from the Furiosa AI, Intel, Apple, NVIDIA, Macronix, and Mozilla team. Fur-

div.head: A.1. Action Space
div.p: Our agents use a minimal action space with 8 tools designed for web navigation:

• click(element id): Click on a DOM element by its unique identifier • type text(element id, text): Enter text into an input field without submitting • hover(element id): Hover over an element to reveal tooltips or dropdown menus • scroll(direction): Scroll the page up or down to reveal more content • select dropdown option(element id, value): Select an option from a dropdown menu • search(element id, text): Type text and submit (equivalent to type + Enter) • go back(): Navigate to the previous page in browser history • exit(message): Terminate the episode with a final answer or status message All element-targeting tools require an element id parameter that corresponds to an injected integer id attribute in the DOM. This simplifies element identification compared to CSS selectors or XPaths.

div.head: A.2. Error Handling and Validation
div.p: The agent framework includes several error checks that provide feedback during the retry loop:

• MustCallExactlyOneToolCheck: Ensures exactly one function call per step • InvalidActionSchemaCheck: Validates that function arguments match the expected schema • ElementMustExistCheck: Verifies that referenced element IDs exist in the current DOM • MustProvideReasoningCheck: Requires the agent to provide reasoning in the analysis channel • RepeatingActionLoopCheck: Detects and prevents repeated ineffective actions When a check fails, the agent receives an error message and has up to 5 retry attempts to produce a valid action.

div.head: A.3. Conversation Format
div.p: Each agent turn follows a structured format with the GPT-OSS Harmony interface:

1. System message: Establishes agent role and channel usage 2. Developer message: Provides detailed instructions and constraints 3. User message: Contains the task, current HTML state, and any error feedback 4. Assistant response: Analysis in the analysis channel, function call in commentary 5. Tool observation: Result of the action with updated HTML state The conversation history preserves prior reasoning and actions but only includes full HTML in the most recent tool observation to manage context length. This allows the agent to reference its own reasoning trajectory while grounding decisions in the current page state.

div.head: B. Agent Prompts
div.p: This section provides the key prompts used in our system components.

div.head: B.1. Base Agent (ReAct Executor)
div.p: The base agent uses a channel-based prompting format with separate channels for reasoning (analysis) and action emission (commentary). The system prompt establishes the agent's role:

You are a web-execution agent that completes browser tasks by emitting function calls in each step. How each step works: The developer prompt provides detailed instructions for element targeting, function-calling discipline, and recovery from errors:
Element targeting via numeric id (must-follow):

• The environment injects a special id attribute that is an integer string (e.g., ''15''). • When a function requires an element, supply this integer id exactly as shown in the latest HTML. • Do not provide CSS selectors, XPaths, inner text, or made-up ids.
Recovery and loop-avoidance:
• Do not repeat an ineffective action more than once.
• After an error or no-op, re-analyze the latest HTML before choosing a different action.

div.head: B.2. Semantic Deduplicator
div.p: The semantic deduplicator clusters equivalent actions before voting. Its prompt instructs:
You are a deterministic semantic action deduplicator for a web-navigation system.

div.head: Context:
div.p: • All inputs in one call are the same action type (TYPE | SEARCH | STOP).
• All inputs act on the same element target (already checked upstream).
• Your task: group semantically equivalent actions and pick a representative per group.
What ''equivalent'' means:

• Same intent after trivial normalization (case, spacing, punctuation) and minor paraphrase/synonymy. • Do NOT merge items that change intent/topic (e.g., ''apple store'' ̸ = ''apple id login''). Analysis. RSA with N=16, K=4, T=4 achieves 43.6%, comparable to single-round arbitration (44.0%) but at ∼7× the compute cost per step. The marginal benefit of additional aggregation rounds is minimal: increasing T from 2 to 4 improves performance by only 0-1.6% while roughly doubling compute. We hypothesize that RSA's iterative refinement, designed for single-shot reasoning tasks where solutions can be progressively improved, does not transfer well to per-step action selection where the "correct" answer depends heavily on environmental context that cannot be improved through reasoning alone.

PlanRSA performs substantially worse than all baselines (35.2% vs. 43.2% for majority vote). Notably, the selection method within PlanRSA (arbiter vs. random) makes no difference, suggesting the bottleneck is the plan aggregation process itself rather than final selection. This may reflect error accumulation: aggregating plans introduces noise at a higher level of abstraction, and errors in plan formulation propagate to all downstream actions.

div.head: H. Threshold Sensitivity Analysis
div.p: Tables 9 and 10 present the full threshold sweep results for CATTS on WebArena-Lite across different values of N ∈ {3, 5, 10, 20}. Both entropy-gated and margin-gated variants show robust performance across a range of thresholds τ ∈ {0.2, 0.3, . . . , 0.8}. The best-performing threshold for each N is highlighted in bold. The results demonstrate that CATTS is relatively robust to the choice of threshold across different values of N. For entropy-gated CATTS, the best thresholds vary by N but most configurations outperform the majority vote baseline. For margin-gated CATTS, similar robustness is observed with peak performance typically at τ ∈ {0.4, 0.5, 0.7, 0.8}. In practice, a default threshold of τ = 0.5 provides good performance across both benchmarks without extensive tuning.

div.head: I. Vote Distribution Analysis
div.p: Figure 6 presents the detailed analysis of vote distributions across all decision steps from our experiments. This analysis provides empirical support for the two-regime interpretation discussed in Section 4.4.

The distributions reveal several key patterns: (1) Right-skewed top-1 probability: Approximately 42% of steps exhibit neardeterministic consensus with top-1 probability exceeding 0.9, indicating that many decisions fall into a redundant regime.

div.head: Base Model Arbiter Model
div.p: Click "Pantry Staples"
Query: Buy highest rated meat substitute product. Thought: The majority action is too generic. Clicking "Pantry Staples" will take me to the meat substitutes.

div.head: Scroll Down
div.p: Scroll Down Scroll Down

Figure 7. Arbiter failure on high-consensus step. Example trajectory where the sampled candidates exhibit strong consensus (e.g., 9/10 votes for one action), but the arbiter overrides the majority and selects a minority action, leading to task failure. This illustrates that arbitration can be harmful when vote-derived uncertainty is already low.
